subject: Physics
q: Primary coil of a transformer is connected to 220 V AC. Primary and secondary turns of the transforms are 100 and 10 respectively. Secondary coil of transformer is connected to two series resistances as shown in figure. The output voltage V0 is :
question: Primary coil of a transformer is connected to 220 V AC. Primary and secondary turns of the transforms are 100 and 10 respectively. Secondary coil of transformer is connected to two series resistances as shown in figure. The output voltage V0 is :
solution:
Ratio of EMF developed will be given by,

εpεs=NpNs=10010⇒εs=22 V

Therefore, current in secondary circuit will be

I=2222×103=1 mA

Hence, potential drop across 7 kΩ will be V0=I×7 kΩ=7 V

subject: Physics
q: An inductor of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mH</mi></math>, a capacitor of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>200</mn><mo> </mo><mi>μF</mi></math> and a resistor of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> are connected in series with a <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>220</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> ac source. If the current is in phase with the emf, the frequency of ac source will be ______<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>2</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi>Hz</mi></math>.
question: An inductor of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mH</mi></math>, a capacitor of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>200</mn><mo> </mo><mi>μF</mi></math> and a resistor of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> are connected in series with a <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>220</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> ac source. If the current is in phase with the emf, the frequency of ac source will be ______<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>2</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi>Hz</mi></math>.
solution:
Given here: L=0.5×10-3 H

C=200×10-6 F

R=2 Ω.

If current is in phase with emf, so this is the condition of resonance.

f=fR=12πLC=12×3.14LC

f=16.280.5×10-3×200×10-6=503.55 Hz=5.03×102 Hz

question: A 20 H inductor coil is connected to a 10 Ω resistance in series as shown in figure. The time at which rate of dissipation of energy (Joule's heat) across resistance is equal to the rate at which magnetic energy is stored in the inductor, is:
solution:
Rate of dissipation of energy in resistor =i2R
Rate of energy stored in inductor =ddt12Li2=Lididt
i2R=Lididt
didt=iRL      …i
In L-R  circuit:

i=i01-e-tτ              (∴ τ=LR=2)
didt=i0τe-t/τ
From equation (i),

i0τe-t/τ=i01-e-tτRL
e-t/τ=1-e-t/τ
e-tτ=12  ⇒t=τ ln2
=2 ln2

q: An ideal capacitor of capacitance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.2</mo><mo> </mo><mi>μF</mi></math> is charged to a potential difference of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>. The charging battery is then disconnected. The capacitor is then connected to an ideal inductor of self inductance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mH</mi></math>. The current at a time when the potential difference across the capacitor is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> is:
question: An ideal capacitor of capacitance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.2</mo><mo> </mo><mi>μF</mi></math> is charged to a potential difference of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>. The charging battery is then disconnected. The capacitor is then connected to an ideal inductor of self inductance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mH</mi></math>. The current at a time when the potential difference across the capacitor is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> is:
solution:
The energy stored in the capacitor is given by, Uc=12CV2. The energy stored in the inductor is given by. UL=12LI2. In LC circuit there is no loss of energy in the form of heat so, using energy conservation, Uci+ULi=Ucf+ULf

⇒ 12×0.2×10-6×102+0=12×0.2×10-6×52+12×0.5×10-3I2
I= 3×10-1A=0.17 A

subject: Physics
q: A series LCR circuit with $R = 20\ \Omega$, $L = 1.6\text{ H}$ and $C = 40\ \mu\text{F}$ is connected to a variable frequency a.c. source. The inductive reactance at resonant frequency is _______ $\Omega$.
question: A series LCR circuit with $R = 20\ \Omega$, $L = 1.6\text{ H}$ and $C = 40\ \mu\text{F}$ is connected to a variable frequency a.c. source. The inductive reactance at resonant frequency is _______ $\Omega$.
solution:
Given $L = 1.6\text{ H}$ and $C = 40\ \mu\text{F} = 40 \times 10^{-6}\text{ F}$.

At resonance, the angular frequency is $\omega_r = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{LC}}$.

The inductive reactance at resonance is $X_L = \omega_r L = \dfrac{L}{\sqrt{LC}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{L}{C}}$.

Substituting the given values:

$X_L = \sqrt{\dfrac{1.6}{40 \times 10^{-6}}}$

$X_L = \sqrt{\dfrac{1.6}{4 \times 10^{-5}}}$

$X_L = \sqrt{4 \times 10^4}$

$X_L = 200\ \Omega$

Answer: $200$

q: In a series <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>LR</mi></math> circuit, power of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>400</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">W</mi></math> is dissipated from a source of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>250</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>50</mn><mo> </mo><mi>Hz</mi></math>. Taking the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mfrac><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mrow><mn>3</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">π</mi></mrow></mfrac></mfenced><mo> </mo><mi>μF</mi></math>, then value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi></math> is
question: In a series <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>LR</mi></math> circuit, power of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>400</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">W</mi></math> is dissipated from a source of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>250</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>50</mn><mo> </mo><mi>Hz</mi></math>. The power factor of the circuit is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>8</mn></math>. In order to bring the power factor to unity, a capacitor of value <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> is added in series to the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">L</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi></math>. Taking the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mfrac><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mrow><mn>3</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">π</mi></mrow></mfrac></mfenced><mo> </mo><mi>μF</mi></math>, then value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi></math> is
solution:
P=Vm. in cosϕ

400=250×1m×0.8

irms=2A

1m2.R=P

4×R=400

⇒ R=100Ω.

cosϕ=RR2+XL2

1002+XL2=1000.82

1002+XL2=1000.82

XL=75Ω

Power factor is unity

XC=XL=75

1ω=75

⇒ C=175×2H×50=17500πF

3π×2500

=13π×4×102 mF

=4003πμF

N=400

subject: Physics
q: When resonance is produced in a series LCR circuit, then which of the following is not correct?
question: When resonance is produced in a series LCR circuit, then which of the following is not correct?
options: Current in the circuit is in phase with the applied voltage.
Inductive and capacitive reactances are equal.
If $\mathrm{R}$ is reduced, the voltage across capacitor will increase.
Impedance of the circuit is maximum.
solution:
Impedance $(Z)$ of the series LCR circuit is
$Z=\sqrt{R^2+\left(X_L-X_C\right)^2}$
At resonance, $\mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{L}}=\mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{C}}$
Therefore, $Z_{\text {minimum }}=R$

For maximum current in series LCR circuit,

XC=XL⇒12πfC=2πfL

⇒C=14π2f2 L=14×π2×49×106×2×10-6

⇒C=13872 F

Therefore, x=3872.
